content: A black hole is an invisible region in space where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape from it. Imagine a star much larger than our Sun that has run out of fuel and collapsed under its own weight. This collapse creates a point of extreme density called a singularity, surrounded by a boundary known as the event horizon. The event horizon marks the “point of no return.” Anything that crosses this boundary—whether it’s light, matter, or information—is pulled inexorably inward and cannot get out. The intense gravity is due to the star’s mass being squeezed into an incredibly small space, creating a gravitational field far stronger than anything we experience on Earth. Black holes are important because they challenge our understanding of physics and help us learn about the universe’s most extreme conditions. Despite their name, black holes are not holes in space but rather objects with gravitational pulls so powerful that escape is impossible.
